lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms repeatedly packaging client-owned products, including kit assembly, blister packaging, shrink wrapping, folding, labeling, imprinting, inspection, and related handling for external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Manufacturers that process materials into a different product, packaging-material manufacturers, captive in-house packaging lines, pure fulfillment or warehousing providers outside NAICS 561910, one-off consumer gift wrapping without repeat outsourced revenue, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Brand owners and manufacturers outsource runs under per-unit, per-batch, project, minimum-volume, setup, materials-pass-through, and recurring program arrangements; the provider supplies labor, packaging machinery, floor space, quality control, labeling data, and changeover capability around client-owned goods.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupational employment for the broader NAICS 561900 group, while O*NET describes packaging-machine and hand-packing occupations across industries and PMMI surveys CPG, OEM, warehouse, and contract-packaging participants. I map repetitive picking, feeding, packing, labeling, counting, visual inspection, line monitoring, materials movement, production records, scheduling, and quality documentation to AI-enabled robotics, machine vision, optimization, and administrative automation, while retaining irregular dexterous handling, sanitation, changeovers, maintenance, exception resolution, customer coordination, and already-automated machine cycles. The four-digit occupation table underrepresents 561910 production work and therefore biases exposure downward; treating mature conventional machinery as new AI opportunity would bias it upward.
a.rationale: Packaging work contains repetitive and observable physical sequences plus standardized records that are technically exposed to robotics, machine vision, and AI-assisted control. O*NET also shows substantial hands-on inspection, material handling, jam clearing, cleaning, adjustment, and packaging-machine tending, so exposure is well below full labor substitution. PMMI identifies machine vision, predictive maintenance, repetitive-task automation, and robotic package handling as active opportunities, but much installed machinery already embodies conventional automation and is excluded from the current opportunity.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit staffing and wage table for NAICS 561910 combined with time-and-motion studies at contract packagers that separate already-automated cycles from manual handling, inspection, changeover, maintenance, supervision, data work, and customer service would materially change this estimate.
caveats: No six-digit occupation mix or task-time study was found for NAICS 561910.
NAICS 561900 includes unrelated other support services, while O*NET occupations span manufacturers and warehouses as well as contract packagers.
The line between conventional packaging automation already in place and incremental AI-enabled substitution is not measured.

rho.bridge: PMMI measures automation plans and operating constraints among CPG warehouses and packaging-sector participants, and MHI/Deloitte measures five-year technology intentions across manufacturing and supply-chain leaders rather than realized labor conversion at lower-middle-market contract packagers. I translate those adoption intentions into a five-year implementation share, then haircut for capital cost, facility layout, integration, product variability, sanitation and regulated-customer qualification, downtime during installation, maintenance skills, and the need to keep lines flexible for short runs. Larger survey respondents and intention-versus-realization bias likely push the proxy upward; acute labor shortages and modular robots available to contract packagers may push it downward.
rho.rationale: PMMI reports warehouse automation rising but still limited, with less than 20% of surveyed CPG firms mostly automated and very few fully automated. Its packaging research describes AI as early pilots and highlights interoperability, training, maintenance, and startup collaboration. MHI/Deloitte reports broad five-year plans for AI and robotics, indicating diffusion pressure, but physical retrofits and diverse client runs make full conversion unlikely within five years.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year cohort of US contract packaging plants reporting capital projects, commissioning dates, uptime, line changeovers, client validation, baseline and post-install labor hours, maintenance staffing, rework, and avoided hiring by workflow would materially change the value.
caveats: Technology adoption intentions are not realized labor savings.
The cited surveys cover broader CPG, manufacturing, warehousing, and supply-chain populations and skew larger than the screened firms.
Flexible short runs and regulated food, pharmaceutical, or medical-device work can make integration and validation slower than broad adoption headlines imply.

e.rationale: The official definition is already an external contract service applied to client-owned materials, so captive status is less important than recurrence, customer concentration, operating independence, and whether the provider is truly a service firm rather than a misclassified manufacturer or warehouse. The main exclusions are one-off or consumer-oriented work, firms dependent on one program, unsafe or noncompliant plants, obsolete equipment, weak customer approvals, nonassignable contracts, and businesses whose customer relationships or technical know-how leave with one owner.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census in the screened earnings band coding repeat-program revenue, customer concentration, contract assignability, regulatory approvals, equipment ownership, service-versus-manufacturing classification, and management depth would materially change the estimate.
caveats: No source measures eligibility under the specified earnings and recurring-service lens.
NAICS 561910 ranges from simple gift wrapping and folding to regulated blister packaging and complex multi-SKU kitting.
The frozen firm count is margin-bridged from size classes and may misstate membership for capital-intensive or pass-through-heavy operators.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year sell-or-transfer intentions among all US employer-business owners, while Capstone measures transaction activity in the much broader print, paper, packaging, and equipment sector. I anchor below Gallup's 22% intention rate because intentions do not equal completed qualifying control transfers, then allow support from strategic consolidation and operational-improvement demand while excluding internal transfers, minority financings, product manufacturers, and firms too concentrated or obsolete to transfer. Broad intentions likely bias completed probability upward; packaging-specific strategic buyer activity may make a general-owner anchor conservative for strong assets.
s5.rationale: Gallup finds that 22% of employer-business owners plan a sale or ownership transfer within five years. Capstone shows packaging buyers remain active but selective, favoring resilient niches, operational improvements, scale, and technology differentiation; current transaction volume is subdued. A qualifying transfer is therefore plausible but remains a minority outcome for eligible firms.
s5.change_evidence: A PitchBook, GF Data, or DealStats cohort matched to a denominator of eligible US NAICS 561910 firms, with control status, EBITDA, failed processes, buyer type, customer concentration, and service-versus-manufacturing classification, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Gallup measures intentions rather than completed transfers and covers all employer businesses.
Capstone combines packaging products, paper, print, equipment, and services and does not provide a 561910 denominator.
Customer concentration, plant condition, and required reinvestment can prevent an apparent packaging business from completing a durable control transfer.

q.rationale: Per-unit, per-batch, setup, and fixed-program pricing can let a provider retain labor and throughput gains until renewal, and faster lines can increase capacity without proportionate hiring. Retention is reduced by competitive rebids, open-book or cost-plus provisions, customer demands for productivity sharing, minimum-volume shortfalls, materials pass-through, capital depreciation, integration and maintenance expense, and the need to preserve spare capacity for volatile client schedules. Benefits tied to released capacity have limited value unless demand fills that capacity.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level data from independent contract packagers showing revenue by per-unit, per-batch, time-and-materials, cost-plus, setup, and fixed-fee terms, together with automation capital costs, renewal repricing, customer gain-sharing, utilization, and realized contribution-margin changes, would materially change the range.
caveats: No source directly measures billing mix or retained automation benefit in lower-middle-market NAICS 561910 firms.
Customer contracts may combine service fees with materials pass-through and capital recovery in ways that obscure the retained share.
Throughput gains do not become cash benefit when released line capacity remains idle or requires additional selling and working capital.

d5.bridge: BLS projects constant-dollar output for the broader NAICS 561900 group, while PMMI's contract packaging/manufacturing survey measures outsourcing intentions across a broader North American CP/CM market. I use the broader group's 2.4% annual real-output projection as the central growth anchor, cross-check it against brand-owner plans to maintain or increase outsourcing, and haircut for weak consumer volumes, insourcing, customer loss, packaging reduction, and 561900's unrelated services. The broad output series may bias either way; CP/CM respondents' expansion expectations and inclusion of contract manufacturing likely bias growth upward for pure 561910 services.
d5.rationale: BLS projects NAICS 561900 real output to rise from $72.2 billion in 2024 to $91.8 billion in 2034, a 2.4% compound annual rate. PMMI reporting says 67% of brand owners plan to maintain or increase outsourced CP/CM use and three-quarters of providers expect significant three-year expansion, supported by specialized formats, new-product tests, surge capacity, and e-commerce. Consumer-volume pressure, sustainability-driven material reduction, insourcing, and economic cyclicality keep the downside near flat.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year Census or BEA revenue and price indexes for NAICS 561910, separated into physical volume, service mix, materials pass-through, and regulated versus discretionary end markets, plus a repeated US brand-owner outsourcing survey, would materially change the range.
caveats: The BLS output series is four-digit and includes unrelated other support services.
The PMMI survey combines contract packaging with contract manufacturing and covers North American participants rather than a 561910 probability sample.
Packaging demand depends on underlying client volumes, while sustainability rules can either create relabeling and reformatting work or reduce material and service intensity.

o.bridge: The Census definition establishes that the current service physically packages client-owned products, and PMMI surveys broader contract packaging/manufacturing buyers about why they outsource rather than measuring year-five operator-required share for NAICS 561910. I treat physical handling, machinery access, surge capacity, short-run customization, quality control, and regulated execution as requiring an accountable operator, while allowing elimination through packaging reduction, client insourcing, standardized self-service equipment, or migration to integrated contract manufacturers. Combining CP with manufacturing may overstate the need for the narrower operator; the physical and compliance-bound nature of packaging makes general software substitution less relevant and may make the estimate conservative.
o.rationale: Software can automate scheduling, records, label creation, and inspection decisions, but cannot itself receive and physically package client goods. PMMI reports buyers use contractors to access machinery and formats unavailable in-house, test products, customize short runs, and supplement capacity. Most surviving quantity therefore still needs a plant operator, although brands can buy equipment, consolidate into full-service manufacturers, or remove packaging.
o.change_evidence: A longitudinal survey of US brand owners tracking outsourced packaging spend retained, insourced, eliminated, moved to contract manufacturers, or replaced by self-service equipment by service line and end market would materially change the value.
caveats: No source directly measures substitution of external packaging-service purchases after automation.
PMMI combines contract packaging and manufacturing, which may have different switching costs and operator requirements.
Simple labeling, folding, and gift wrapping face more insourcing and self-service risk than regulated blister packaging or complex kitting.

narrative.executive_view: Packaging and labeling services offer a meaningful but capital-dependent automation opportunity. Repetitive handling, inspection, labeling, records, and line control are exposed to robotics, machine vision, and AI optimization, while physical execution, changeovers, maintenance, quality accountability, and client-specific compliance keep an operator central.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can improve machine vision, defect detection, label verification, production scheduling, demand and material planning, predictive maintenance, work instructions, traceability, and administrative records. AI-enabled robots and autonomous handling can reduce manual picking, packing, palletizing, and line feeding, but diverse shapes, flexible materials, short runs, sanitation, jams, and frequent changeovers preserve hands-on work.
narrative.value_capture: Per-unit and program pricing can retain labor and throughput gains until contracts are rebid, especially when automation avoids scarce hiring or adds sellable capacity. Customer gain-sharing, price competition, materials pass-through, integration and maintenance cost, depreciation, idle capacity, and minimum-volume volatility reduce retained benefit.
narrative.firm_availability: The NAICS definition is inherently outsourced, but eligible firms still need recurring programs, diversified customers, assignable contracts, safe and compliant plants, maintained equipment, and management depth. General succession intentions and strategic packaging consolidation support transfer flow, though direct 561910 transaction evidence is absent and current packaging M&A is selective.
narrative.demand_durability: Brand owners outsource to access specialized equipment and formats, test launches, customize short runs, and handle capacity surges. Broader real-output projections and outsourcing surveys imply growth, while consumer weakness, insourcing, packaging reduction, and shifts to integrated contract manufacturers create downside.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest gaps are six-digit occupation and task-time data, the already-automated baseline, plant-level automation realization, contract pricing, and a qualifying-transfer denominator. A poor outcome would combine expensive or unreliable retrofits with customer concentration, unused capacity, rebid price leakage, regulatory failures, packaging-volume weakness, or clients taking work in-house.
narrative.principal_driver: Repetitive physical handling and inspection can be compressed with AI-enabled robotics and machine vision while customers continue outsourcing for equipment, flexibility, and accountable execution.
narrative.principal_weakness: Automation requires capital and plant integration, and gains can be stranded by short-run variability, idle capacity, customer concentration, or competitive repricing.
